item.title: Other wondermen...,
item.content:encoded:
It’s a very generic name. Besides the Marvel character, Wonderman is the name of a libidinously motivated “superhero” from the show TV Funhouse. Unlike SCP 4640-3 this Wonderman “fights a constant crusade to stop crime, and get his alias laid.”
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TV_Funhouse
A sample episode warning:mildly NSFW
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xruHo6oB_4I

Hopefully this cartoon doesn’t exist in universe otherwise who knows what 4340-3 might do if learning about this Wonderman distresses 4340-1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well maybe the kid hasn't heard of him, or doesn't care. It's a pretty generic name anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so awesome. My favorite part:

Test #: 5

Procedures: Agent Phoros poses as a substitute teacher and attempts to confiscate SCP-4640-2 from SCP-4640-1.

Results SCP-4640-1 responded by taking SCP-4640-2 from its hoodie and placing it inside its pocket, apologizing for wearing the object.

It's so real. And funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to make sure you're aware, there is a Marvel superhero named Wonder Man. All it really means is that the character/Marvel doesn't exist in this articles continuity, but I don't know if that's something you intended to be the case. In other words, I'm not really sure if it's an issue, but you know about it now and can decide for yourself (assuming you read this).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved the description of Wonder Man and the incident log. How he was trying his best to stop the horrid n o t h i n g attacking 4640-1.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for reading and liking my article! Here are some clarifications.

1) It's not much of a redaction but rather more of a reminder that the kid is still part of the SCP and thus should be referred to by staff as such. It'd be bad practice for the Foundation to let personnel grow attached to SPCs.

2) tbh I just felt like having the tests be spaced out like that would help convey there was a big time span between significant test results. That way it doesn't read as if everything happen in a week. The D-class just got replaced in between tests

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a few minor continuity errors I wanted to point out

The scp's first name is mentioned in the interview log but redacted in the testing log where the agent poses as a gorcery store clerk.

Also, why does the test for example go from #10 to #16 but the d class go from 9022 to 9023? I don't even know why it bothers me… it just does.

Phenomenal article, but please fix!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from agreeing to a few posts already made here, I liked this one. Though all I really got from this while reading is a voice in the back of my head screaming "That is 100% a Stand."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
followed by the agent being forced verbally to sit at a corner of the class

This is unclear. Did SCP-4640-3's words anomalously force the agent to move? That should be explained with more detail if that's the case, and this ability could be added to the list at the top of the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing I wanna note, in the interview with Andrew. He's an American kid, so he wouldn't say "maths", he'd say "math".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's that this wouldn't be a problem if the Foundation left it alone.

It's an orphaned kid with a pocket superhero. Merely keeping an eye on him (the entity even has amnesticising abilities) would be enough, since it's mostly self containing.

It's the Foundation pushing up against it that causing it to become more dangerous. The Foundation's policy and approach to ethics is making life harder for them than it needs to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
just wanted to tell you it says "pigmentation" twice.

+1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: also with the camera report, it's Cheerios not Cherios

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was engaged the entire time, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the section 'Discovery', were, beverege, amnestecized, invloved. There's another amnestecized later on.
In the interview, thumb ticks.
Test #16, grovery.
Test #23, braking.

I think that's it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you please point out where they are so I can fix them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I think there's something wonderfully fucked up about posing as his parents that makes this stand out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did Brainy Brian have something to do with this?

Seriously, this is a decent exploration of the liminal space where heroes and imaginary friends blur into each other. There are a few SPAG errors, but they didn't distract from the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me or is -3 hiding something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is a little generic. There's nothing outright wrong with that, but it could be much more interesting. What you've essentially created is a generic superhero, with its own merchandise and everything. You even list generic superhero abilities. I'm not going to personally down vote this, but don't be surprised if people do simply because, like I said, it is very generic.

item.title: Wonderman is here to save the day! Well, only Andrew's day...
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to The Great Hippo for the feedback on this here thing.

I feel like this is way better than my previous two articles, but only time will tell, I guess. Still stayin' positive though!
